cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer to this question. The question is asking what we can infer from the headline "Safina goes from basket case to top of the world." The options are A, B, C, and D. First, I need to understand the context around the headline. The article mentions that Dinara Safina was previously overshadowed by her brother Marat Safin. She has now become the world No. 1 player. The headline in the Sydney Morning Herald during the Australian Open is given as "Safina goes from basket case to top of the world." A "basket case" is an idiom that I think refers to someone who is very nervous, anxious, or emotionally unstable. So the headline is probably saying that Safina was initially very nervous or unstable in her emotional state but then managed to become the top player in the world. Looking at the options: A) Safina wanted to top the world in tennis when she was a baby in a basket. That seems unlikely because "basket case" isn't about being in a basket as a baby. It's more about being emotionally unstable. B) After she won this year's Australian Open, Dinara Safina cried. The headline doesn't mention crying specifically, just the transformation from being a basket case to the top. So maybe not B. C) Too nervous at first, but Safina finally gained confidence and won. This fits with the idea of a "basket case" (nervous/anxious) leading to becoming successful. So this could be the right answer. D) Safina learnt from the basket case and finally became top of the world. The phrase "basket case" here isn't referring to someone she learned from, but rather her own state. So D doesn't make much sense. Also, the article mentions that Safina is emotionally explosive, and the headline is contrasting her volatile emotional state ("mad as a snake") with her eventual success. So the headline is highlighting her
